epigraph.text:
Great is the Lord, and greatly to be praised, and his 
greatness is unsearchable.

content.text:
Can we integrate to infinity? Can we integrate a function that blows up to
infinity within our interval? These questions sound absurd at first—the
definite integral $\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx$ requires a *finite* interval
and a *bounded* function. But consider the region under the curve
$y = 1/x^{2}$ from $x = 1$ to the right, stretching forever toward
infinity. Intuition says this region has infinite area. But let us
compute:
\[
 \defint{1}{b}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx
 = \left[-\frac{1}{x}\right]_{1}^{b}
 = -\frac{1}{b} + 1
 = 1 - \frac{1}{b}.
\]
As $b \to \infty$, this approaches $1$. The infinite region has
*finite area*!

This is the world of **improper integrals**: integrals that extend the
definite integral to handle infinite intervals or unbounded integrands. The
key idea is always the same—replace the "improper" feature by a limit.

content.title: Improper Integral --- Type 1
content.content:
1. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,\infty)$, then
 \[
 \defint{a}{\infty}f(x)\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx,
 \]
 provided the limit exists.

2. If $f$ is continuous on $(-\infty,b]$, then
 \[
 \defint{-\infty}{b}f(x)\dx
 = \limto{a\to-\infty}\defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx.
 \]

3. If $f$ is continuous on $(-\infty,\infty)$, then
 \[
 \defint{-\infty}{\infty}f(x)\dx
 = \defint{-\infty}{c}f(x)\dx + \defint{c}{\infty}f(x)\dx
 \]
 for any real number $c$, provided *both* integrals converge.
If the limit exists and is finite, the integral **converges**.
Otherwise, it **diverges**.

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x}\dx$
content.solution:
\[
 \defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\defint{1}{b}\frac{1}{x}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\bigl[\ln x\bigr]_{1}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\ln b = \infty.
\]
The integral diverges. Despite the curve $1/x$ approaching zero as
$x \to \infty$, the area under it is infinite.

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{-\infty}{\infty}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx$
content.solution:
Split at $c = 0$:
\[
 \defint{-\infty}{\infty}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx
 = \defint{-\infty}{0}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx
 + \defint{0}{\infty}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx.
\]
For the second integral:
\[
 \defint{0}{\infty}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\bigl[\arctan x\bigr]_{0}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}\arctan b = \frac{\pi}{2}.
\]
By symmetry (or direct computation), the first integral also equals
$\pi/2$. Therefore,
\[
 \defint{-\infty}{\infty}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\dx = \pi.
\]
This remarkable identity—the area under the "witch of Agnesi" curve is
exactly $\pi$—connects the geometry of the circle to the algebra of
rational functions.

content.text:
Even on a finite interval, a function may have a vertical asymptote. We
handle these by approaching the point of discontinuity as a limit.

content.title: Improper Integral --- Type 2
content.content:
1. If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b)$ and discontinuous at $b$, then
 \[
 \defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx
 = \limto{t\to b^{-}}\defint{a}{t}f(x)\dx.
 \]

2. If $f$ is continuous on $(a,b]$ and discontinuous at $a$, then
 \[
 \defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx
 = \limto{t\to a^{+}}\defint{t}{b}f(x)\dx.
 \]

3. If $f$ has a discontinuity at $c \in (a,b)$, then
 \[
 \defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx
 = \defint{a}{c}f(x)\dx + \defint{c}{b}f(x)\dx,
 \]
 provided both improper integrals converge.

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}{1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\dx$
content.solution:
The integrand has a vertical asymptote at $x = 0$. We approach from the
right:
$$
 \defint{0}{1}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\dx
 &= \limto{t\to 0^{+}}\defint{t}{1}x^{-1/2}\dx
 = \limto{t\to 0^{+}}\bigl[2\sqrt{x}\,\bigr]_{t}^{1} \\[4pt]
 &= \limto{t\to 0^{+}}(2 - 2\sqrt{t})
 = 2.
$$

content.title: $\displaystyle\defint{0}{3}\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2/3}}\dx$
content.solution:
The integrand has a vertical asymptote at $x = 1$ (inside the interval).
Split:
\[
 \defint{0}{3}\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2/3}}\dx
 = \defint{0}{1}\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2/3}}\dx
 + \defint{1}{3}\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2/3}}\dx.
\]
For the first piece:
\[
 \limto{t\to 1^{-}}\defint{0}{t}(x-1)^{-2/3}\dx
 = \limto{t\to 1^{-}}\bigl[3(x-1)^{1/3}\bigr]_{0}^{t}
 = \limto{t\to 1^{-}}\bigl(3(t-1)^{1/3} - 3(-1)^{1/3}\bigr)
 = 0 + 3 = 3.
\]
For the second piece:
\[
 \limto{t\to 1^{+}}\defint{t}{3}(x-1)^{-2/3}\dx
 = \limto{t\to 1^{+}}\bigl[3(x-1)^{1/3}\bigr]_{t}^{3}
 = 3(2)^{1/3} - 0 = 3\sqrt[3]{2}.
\]
Therefore, $\defint{0}{3}\frac{1}{(x-1)^{2/3}}\dx = 3 + 3\sqrt[3]{2}$.

content.title: Hidden discontinuities
content.content:
Always check for vertical asymptotes *inside* the interval of
integration! A common error is to compute
$\defint{-1}{1}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx = [-1/x]_{-1}^{1} = -1 - 1 = -2$
without noticing the asymptote at $x = 0$. Since $1/x^{2} > 0$, a negative
"answer" is obviously wrong. The correct conclusion: this integral
diverges.

content.text:
The integrals $\int_{1}^{\infty}x^{-p}\dx$ arise so frequently that their
convergence behavior deserves its own theorem.

content.content:
For $p \neq 1$:
\[
 \defint{1}{b}x^{-p}\dx
 = \left[\frac{x^{-p+1}}{-p+1}\right]_{1}^{b}
 = \frac{b^{1-p}}{1-p} - \frac{1}{1-p}.
\]
If $p > 1$, then $1-p < 0$, so $b^{1-p} \to 0$ as $b \to \infty$,
and the integral converges to $\frac{1}{p-1}$.

If $p < 1$, then $1-p > 0$, so $b^{1-p} \to \infty$, and the integral
diverges.

For $p = 1$, we showed in Example [ex:improper-1overx] that the integral
diverges.

content.text:
The borderline case $p = 1$ (the harmonic integral) is the critical
dividing line. Functions that decay faster than $1/x$ (like $1/x^{2}$)
have convergent integrals; those that decay as slowly as $1/x$ or slower do
not.

Often we cannot evaluate an improper integral exactly but still need to know
whether it converges or diverges. The Comparison Test lets us answer this
question by comparing with a simpler function.

content.title: Comparison Test for Improper Integrals
content.content:
Suppose $f$ and $g$ are continuous on $[a,\infty)$ with
$0 \leq f(x) \leq g(x)$ for all $x \geq a$. Then:

1. If $\defint{a}{\infty}g(x)\dx$ converges, then
 $\defint{a}{\infty}f(x)\dx$ converges.

2. If $\defint{a}{\infty}f(x)\dx$ diverges, then
 $\defint{a}{\infty}g(x)\dx$ diverges.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
In short: *smaller than convergent is convergent; bigger than divergent
is divergent.*

content.title: Using the Comparison Test
content.solution:
For $x \geq 1$, $x^{2}+1 > x^{2}$, so
$\dfrac{1}{x^{2}+1} < \dfrac{1}{x^{2}}$.
Since $\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx$ converges (it equals $1$),
the Comparison Test tells us $\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}+1}\dx$
also converges.

content.title: Divergence by comparison
content.solution:
For $x \geq 4$, $\sqrt{x} - 0.5 \leq \sqrt{x}$, so
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x}-0.5} \geq \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{x}} = \dfrac{1}{x^{1/2}}$.
Since $\defint{4}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{1/2}}\dx$ diverges ($p = 1/2 \leq 1$),
the original integral also diverges.

content.text:
Perhaps the most astonishing application of improper integrals is
**Gabriel's Horn** (also called Torricelli's Trumpet). Rotate the
curve $y = 1/x$ for $x \geq 1$ around the $x$-axis to form a
three-dimensional solid.

content.title: Gabriel's Horn
content.problem: Show that Gabriel's Horn has finite volume but infinite surface area.
content.solution:
**Volume.** By the disk method,
\[
 V = \pi\defint{1}{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^{2}\dx
 = \pi\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x^{2}}\dx
 = \pi\cdot 1 = \pi.
\]
The volume is finite: $V = \pi$.

**Surface area.** The surface area formula gives
\[
 S = 2\pi\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x}\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{x^{4}}}\dx.
\]
For $x \geq 1$, $\sqrt{1+1/x^{4}} \geq 1$, so
\[
 S \geq 2\pi\defint{1}{\infty}\frac{1}{x}\dx = \infty.
\]
The surface area is infinite.

content.text:
This is genuinely paradoxical: you could fill Gabriel's Horn with a finite
amount of paint ($\pi$ cubic units), yet you could never paint its surface!
The paradox arises because the paint inside the horn gets infinitely thin as
it extends to infinity, requiring less and less volume per unit length—but
the surface keeps accumulating area.

Improper integrals are indispensable in probability and statistics. A
**probability density function** $f(x)$ satisfies $f(x) \geq 0$ and
\[
 \defint{-\infty}{\infty}f(x)\dx = 1.
\]
The probability that a random variable $X$ falls in the interval $[a,b]$ is
$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \defint{a}{b}f(x)\dx$.

content.title: Exponential distribution
content.problem:
Verify that $f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$ (and $f(x) = 0$
for $x < 0$) is a probability density function for any $\lambda > 0$. Find
the probability that $X \leq 2$ when $\lambda = 1$.
content.solution:
Clearly $f(x) \geq 0$. Check that the total integral is $1$:
\[
 \defint{0}{\infty}\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\dx
 = \lambda\limto{b\to\infty}\left[-\frac{e^{-\lambda x}}{\lambda}
 \right]_{0}^{b}
 = \limto{b\to\infty}(1-e^{-\lambda b}) = 1.\;\checkmark
\]
For $\lambda = 1$:
\[
 P(X \leq 2) = \defint{0}{2}e^{-x}\dx
 = \bigl[-e^{-x}\bigr]_{0}^{2}
 = 1 - e^{-2} \approx 0.8647.
\]

content.title: Evangelista Torricelli (1608--1647)
content.content:
Gabriel's Horn was first studied by Evangelista Torricelli, a student of
Galileo, in 1643. Torricelli computed the volume using Cavalieri's method of
indivisibles—predating the formal development of calculus by Newton and
Leibniz. The result so astonished the mathematical community that it was
initially called "Torricelli's paradox." The name "Gabriel's Horn"
alludes to the archangel Gabriel, whose trumpet announces the resurrection in
Christian tradition.

marginNotes.text:
Great is our Lord, and abundant in power; his
understanding is beyond measure.

My grace is sufficient for you, for my
power is made perfect in weakness.

Can you find out the deep things of God? Can
you find out the limit of the Almighty? It is higher than heaven—what can
you do? Deeper than Sheol—what can you know?

In a moment, in the twinkling of an
eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be
raised imperishable, and we shall be changed.

marginNotes.author: Georg Cantor
marginNotes.text:
The essence of mathematics lies entirely in its
freedom.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text:
The heart has its reasons, which reason does not
know.
